back_translation: He and His disciples crossed the sea to the other side, into the region of Gerasa.
Immediately when He came up from the boat, there was a man who had an unclean spirit dwelling in him, who came out from the burial tomb to meet Him.
That man was living in the burial tombs; no one could bind him any longer, not even with chains and shackles.
Because many times he had been bound with shackles and chains, but he had wrenched the chains apart and broken the shackles to pieces; no one had strength enough to subdue him.
Day and night he was in the burial tombs and on the mountain, crying out and cutting himself with stones.
When he saw Jesus from afar, he ran and bowed down before His presence.
He cried out with a loud voice: 'What is between me and you, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? I swear to you in the name of God: do not torment me!'
For He had said to it: 'Come out of this man, you unclean spirit!'
He asked it: 'What is your name?' It answered: 'My name is Legion, because we are many.'
It begged Him earnestly not to drive them out of that region.
On the hillside there, a great herd of pigs was feeding.
They begged Him: 'Please send us into the herd of pigs, so that we may enter and possess them.'

He gave permission. The unclean spirits came out of the man and entered the pigs. The herd of pigs, about two thousand, ran and plunged down from the cliff into the sea and drowned to death in that sea.
The pig herders fled and reported the matter both in the city and in the countryside. People came out to see what had happened.
They came to Jesus and saw the man who had been demon-possessed sitting, clothed and in his right mind — the same one who had been possessed by Legion — and they were afraid.
Those who had seen the event told them what had happened to the demon-possessed man and to the herd of pigs.
They began to plead with Him to leave their region.
While He was getting into the boat, the man who had been demon-possessed begged Him to be allowed to remain with Him.
But He did not allow it; He said to him: 'Go back to your house, go to your people, and tell them what the Lord has done for you, and how He has had mercy on you.'
The man went and began to proclaim throughout the region of Decapolis what Jesus had done for him, and everyone marveled.
When Jesus had crossed back over the sea by boat to the other side once more, a great crowd gathered around Him while He was at the seashore.
Behold, a synagogue ruler named Jairus came to Him; when he saw Him, he fell down at His feet.
And he pleaded with Him earnestly: 'My little daughter is near to dying. Please come and lay your hand on her, so that she may be healed and live.'
He went with him, and a great crowd followed Him, pressing in around Him.
There was a woman who had been sick with a flow of blood for twelve years.
She had suffered greatly from the treatments of many doctors, had spent all her possessions to the point of being destitute, but had not gotten any better — instead had become worse.
When she had heard about Jesus, she pushed her way into the crowd from behind and touched His garment.
For she had been thinking in her heart: 'If only I touch even His garment, I will be healed.'
Immediately, her flow of blood stopped, and she felt in her body that she was healed of the disease.
Immediately, Jesus knew within Himself that power had gone out from Him, so He turned to the crowd and said: 'Who touched my garment?'
His disciples said: 'You see the crowd pressing in upon You, and yet You ask, "Who touched me?"'
But He kept looking around to see the one who had done this.
As for the woman, knowing what had happened to her, she was trembling with fear, came and fell down before His presence, and told Him the whole truth.
He said to her: 'Daughter, your faith has saved you. Go in peace, and be healed of your disease.'
While He was still speaking, people came from the house of the synagogue ruler, saying: 'Your daughter has died. Why bother the Teacher any further?'
But Jesus heard the words they spoke, and said to the synagogue ruler: 'Do not be afraid; only believe.'
He did not allow anyone to follow Him, except Peter, James, and John the younger brother of James.
They arrived at the house of the synagogue ruler. He saw a commotion, with people crying and wailing loudly.
He went in and said to them: 'Why are you in commotion and weeping? The little child has not died, but is only sleeping.'
They laughed at Him in scorn, but He sent everyone out, then took the father and mother of the child, and those who were with Him, into the place where the child was lying.
He took the child's hand, and said to her: 'Talitha koum,' which means: 'Little girl, I tell you, get up.'
Immediately, the little girl got up and walked around, because she was twelve years old, and they were astonished with great amazement.
He sternly charged them not to let anyone know about this, and commanded that food be brought for the child to eat.
